name: Tally Marks and Pictographs — Class 3 Data
whatIsIt: To collect data we use TALLY MARKS — one stroke per count, with the fifth stroke crossing the bundle (卌) so groups of 5 are easy to count. A PICTOGRAPH then shows the counts with symbols. Sometimes one symbol stands for MORE than one — the KEY tells you (e.g. 1 ⭐ = 2 children), so you multiply.
whyNeeded: Surveys, votes and score-keeping all start with tallies, then become pictures everyone can read. Learning to make tallies and read a key is the bridge from counting to real graphs.
realLifeUses: Tallying votes for the class monitor
Counting vehicles passing the school gate
A pictograph of favourite games in the class
Keeping score in a game with tally marks

vocabulary.meaning: One stroke per count; the 5th crosses the bundle of four.
A chart that shows counts using symbols.
The rule telling what one symbol is worth (e.g. 1 = 2).

story.title: The Favourite-Game Survey
story.text: Sara asks the class their favourite game and makes tally marks: cricket 卌 卌 (10), kho-kho 卌 (5), carrom 卌 || (7). She draws a pictograph where one ⚽ stands for 2 children. 'How many symbols for cricket?' asks the teacher. Sara thinks: 10 children, and 1 symbol = 2, so 10 ÷ 2 = 5 symbols.
story.extractedProblem: Cricket got 10 votes. In a pictograph where 1 symbol = 2 children, how many symbols show cricket?

visual.caption: Survey counts: cricket 10, carrom 7, kho-kho 5. Read the KEY before counting symbols.

teachingGallery.title: Tally, then read the key
teachingGallery.note: 5th tally crosses the bundle. One symbol may stand for many — check the key.

standardMethod.summary: Collect with tallies (bundle by 5), count each group, then read a pictograph using its key.
steps: Make one tally per item; the 5th stroke crosses the bundle of four.
Count each bundle of five plus leftovers.
To read a pictograph: read the KEY first (1 symbol = ?).
Number of things = symbols × key value; symbols needed = count ÷ key value.

workedExamples.problem: A pictograph key says 1 🍎 = 5 apples. Monday shows 4 apple symbols. How many apples?
In a survey, 12 children like cricket. In a pictograph where 1 symbol = 2, how many symbols are needed?

commonMistakes.mistake: Counting each symbol as 1 when the key says one symbol is worth more.
commonMistakes.fix: Read the KEY first. If 1 symbol = 5, then 4 symbols mean 20, not 4.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not crossing the fifth tally, so counting the marks becomes slow and error-prone.
commonMistakes.fix: Every fifth stroke crosses the bundle (卌). Count in fives, then add leftovers.

easy.q: The FIRST thing to read on a pictograph is…
hintLadder: Symbols may be worth more than 1.

medium.q: Key: 1 symbol = 3 books. Tuesday shows 5 symbols. How many books?
Cricket got 10 votes. Key: 1 symbol = 2. How many symbols?

challenge.q: A pictograph: mango 6 symbols, apple 4 symbols. Key 1 = 5. How many MORE mangoes than apples?
Riya sees 3 symbols (key 1 = 10) and writes 'only 3 books'. What did she forget?

questions.q: Tally 卌 卌 means how many?
Key: 1 symbol = 4. Friday shows 6 symbols. How many?
8 children like art. Key 1 symbol = 2. How many symbols?
When making tallies, every FIFTH mark should…
hintLadder: Bundles of five are easy to count.

teachBackPrompt: Run a mini survey at home (favourite fruit or colour of 5–6 people). Record answers with tally marks, then draw a pictograph. Explain your key and how many each symbol stands for.

revisionCard.summary: Tally: one stroke each, 5th crosses the bundle (卌). Pictograph: read the KEY first. Things = symbols × key value; symbols = count ÷ key value. 'How many more' = subtract the real counts.

rememberIt.hook: Each picture stands for a number - check the KEY
rememberIt.unpack: If 1 apple = 10, then 3 apples = 30. Half a picture = half the value. Always read the key first.

studentQuestions.a: One picture often stands for many (1 = 10). The key tells you how many, so you can multiply.

studentQuestions.q: What does half a symbol mean?
studentQuestions.a: Half the value - if a whole star = 10, half a star = 5.
